title: How to Make Decisions Using Your Intuition
meta_description: Learn to trust your gut and make better decisions with your intuition. No fluff, just real guidance for sensitive people.
og_title: How to Make Decisions Using Your Intuition
og_description: Learn to trust your gut and make better decisions with your intuition. No fluff, just real guidance for sensitive people.
body:
You've Been Making Decisions Wrong Your Whole Life

Let's be real for a second. You've probably been taught that good decisions come from logic. From pros-and-cons lists. From spreadsheets and sleep-on-it strategies. And sure, those things have their place. But if you're someone who feels things deeply - someone whose gut twists when something's off, whose body gets heavy in certain rooms, who just *knows* when something's right even if you can't explain why - then you've also been taught that something's wrong with you for feeling that way.

Nothing's wrong with you. You're not broken. You're just wired for a wider range of input than most people are comfortable with. And your intuition isn't some woo-woo fantasy. It's a real, usable decision-making tool that's been running in the background your whole life. You just haven't been shown how to turn up the volume.

So let's talk about how to actually use your intuition to make decisions. Not the vague, "follow your heart" kind of advice that leaves you more confused than when you started. The real, practical, sometimes-uncomfortable kind. The kind that works.

What Intuition Actually Is (And Isn't)

First, let's clear something up. Intuition isn't magic. It's not a psychic hotline to the universe (though sometimes it can feel that way). It's your brain's ability to process information faster than your conscious mind can keep up with. Your nervous system picks up on patterns, micro-expressions, environmental cues, and past experiences - all in a split second - and then sends you a signal. That signal is what you call a "gut feeling."

Think about it like this: You walk into a room and immediately feel uneasy. You can't point to why. The people seem fine. The lighting is normal. But something's off. That's your intuition working. Your brain has processed a thousand tiny details - the way someone's shoulders are tense, the pause between words, the slight shift in eye contact - and it's telling you: pay attention.

Intuition isn't the opposite of logic. It's logic that's running too fast for your conscious mind to track. And when you learn to trust it, you stop second-guessing yourself into paralysis. Right?!

The Problem With "Just Trust Your Gut"

Here's where it gets tricky. You've probably been burned before. You "followed your gut" and it led you straight into a bad relationship, a bad job, or a bad decision. And now you're skeptical. You think: Maybe my intuition is broken. Maybe I'm just making things up. Maybe I'm too emotional.

But here's the thing - that wasn't your intuition. That was fear wearing a Halloween costume. Or wishful thinking. Or old trauma patterns that feel familiar, so your brain mistakes them for "right." Intuition and fear feel different in the body, but most people haven't been taught to tell them apart. Does that land?

Fear feels tight, cold, and urgent. It's the voice that says "you have to decide RIGHT NOW or you'll miss out." It's the knot in your stomach that comes with a sense of scarcity - like there's not enough time, not enough options, not enough you.

Intuition feels different. It's calm. It's patient. It doesn't yell. It doesn't panic. It's the quiet knowing that settles in your chest, even when the logical mind is still screaming for more data. Intuition doesn't need you to act immediately. It just needs you to listen.

The Body Knows Before Your Brain Does

Your body is the most reliable decision-making tool you have. And you've been ignoring it for years. Maybe decades.

Think about a time you made a decision that turned out wrong. Not a small wrong - the kind that cost you sleep, money, or peace. Can you remember what your body felt like right before you made that choice? Chances are, there was a signal. A heaviness in your chest. A tightness in your throat. A sinking feeling in your gut. You felt it. You just didn't trust it.

Now think about a decision that turned out right. The kind that felt like coming home. What did your body feel like then? An openness? A lightness? A sense of expansion?

Your body has been giving you feedback your whole life. You just weren't taught the language. Here's the translation:

  • Tightness, heaviness, contraction = "No" or "Not yet" or "Something's off"
  • Openness, lightness, expansion = "Yes" or "This is aligned" or "Keep going"
  • Numbness, fuzziness, confusion = "You're not ready to decide yet. Get more info or wait."

This isn't complicated. But it takes practice to feel the difference between a "no" and a "not yet" - or between a "yes" and a "this feels exciting because it's dangerous." That's where the work is.

How to Actually Practice Intuitive Decision-Making

You don't learn to trust your intuition by jumping off a cliff and hoping the universe catches you. You learn by starting small. By making low-stakes decisions with your gut and then paying attention to how they turn out. Over time, you build evidence that your intuition is reliable. And that evidence is what lets you trust it when the stakes are high.

Here's a practice you can start today. It takes five minutes. And it will change everything.

The Five-Minute Intuition Check

Next time you have a decision to make - even a small one, like what to eat for lunch or which route to take to work - stop. Close your eyes. Take three slow breaths. Then ask yourself: "What does my body want me to know about this?"

Don't think about it. Don't analyze it. Just feel. Notice where the answer comes from. Does it feel like a pull? A push? A stillness? A resistance? Pay attention to the physical sensation, not the story your mind is telling you about it.

Then make your choice based on that sensation. And here's the key: notice what happens next. Not in a "did I get the outcome I wanted" way, but in a "how does my body feel now that I've decided" way. If you feel relief, you made the right call. If you feel more tension, you probably didn't.

Do this for a week. Just small decisions. You'll start to notice patterns. You'll start to see that your body is actually pretty good at this. And you'll start to trust it more.

Intuition isn't the opposite of logic. It's logic that's running too fast for your conscious mind to track. The real skill isn't choosing between head and heart - it's learning to listen to both at the same time.

The Decision Matrix That Actually Works

Here's a approach I use with people who are stuck. It's not fancy. It's not complicated. But it works because it forces you to check in with both your head and your body before you decide.

Step 1: Get the Facts

Write down what you know. Not what you feel. Not what you're afraid of. Just the facts. What are the options? What are the concrete outcomes? What are the timelines? Get it out of your head and onto paper. This clears the mental clutter so you can actually feel something.

Step 2: Body Scan Each Option

For each option, close your eyes and imagine yourself choosing it. Not the outcome - just the act of choosing. How does your body respond? Does it tighten or open? Does it feel heavy or light? Does it feel like a "yes" or a "no"? Don't judge the response. Just notice it.

If you're struggling to feel anything, try this: put one hand on your belly and one hand on your heart. Take a few breaths. Then ask the question again. Your hands act like antennas. They help you tune in.

Step 3: Check for Fear vs. Intuition

Ask yourself: "Is this feeling familiar because it's right, or because it's what I'm used to?" If the feeling is tight, cold, and urgent, it's probably fear. If it's calm, quiet, and patient - even if it's uncomfortable - it's probably intuition.

Step 4: Make Your Choice and Commit

Once you've checked in with your body and your brain, make the choice. Then commit to it. Don't second-guess. Don't reopen the decision unless new information comes in. The second-guessing is what keeps you stuck, not the choice itself.

If you want a deeper dive into this process, The Gift of Fear by Gavin de Becker is the book that changed how I think about intuition. It's not spiritual at all - it's about how your brain processes danger signals faster than you can think. It's the most practical book on intuition I've ever read. See on Amazon (paid link).

What To Do When Your Intuition Says "No" But Your Logic Says "Yes"

This is where most people get stuck. You've done the work. You've checked your body. Your gut is screaming "no." But your logical brain has a spreadsheet full of reasons why this is the "right" choice. The money is good. The timing is right. Everyone else thinks you should do it.

What do you do?

First, don't ignore the "no." Ever. Your intuition is trying to protect you from something your conscious mind can't see yet. Maybe it's a personality conflict you haven't encountered. Maybe it's a hidden cost. Maybe it's just that the timing is wrong, even though everything else looks right.

Second, don't automatically override your logic either. Your brain has good information too. The goal isn't to pick one over the other. It's to find the intersection.

Ask yourself: "If I follow my intuition's 'no,' what's the worst that happens? Can I handle that? Is there a middle path?" Sometimes the answer is "no" to the whole thing. Sometimes the answer is "not yet" or "not this way" or "only if these conditions are met."

Your intuition is a guide, not a dictator. It's showing you the direction. You still have to walk the path.

Why Your Intuition Gets Louder When You're Stressed

Here's something nobody tells you: your intuition actually gets louder when you're stressed. Not clearer - louder. The signal gets distorted. It's like trying to listen to a radio station during a thunderstorm. The information is still there, but it's mixed with static.

When you're stressed, your nervous system is in survival mode. It's scanning for threats. And it's going to interpret anything unfamiliar as dangerous. So that "no" you're feeling? It might be a real "no." Or it might be your nervous system saying "this is new and I'm scared."

The fix is simple but not easy: calm your nervous system first. Then check your intuition. You can't make good intuitive decisions from a place of panic. You need to be regulated. That means breathing, grounding, moving your body, or whatever else helps you come back to center.

If you struggle with this (and most people do), The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk is essential reading. It explains exactly how your nervous system holds onto trauma and stress - and how that affects your ability to make clear decisions. See on Amazon (paid link).

The One Question That Cuts Through All the Noise

When you're truly stuck - when your head is spinning and your gut is churning and you can't tell what's real - there's one question that cuts through everything. It's simple. It's uncomfortable. And it works.

Ask yourself: "If I knew I couldn't fail, what would I choose?"

But here's the twist: that's not the real question. The real question is: "If I knew I could handle the failure, what would I choose?"

Most of the time, you're not afraid of making the wrong choice. You're afraid of not being able to handle the consequences. You're afraid of the regret, the shame, the judgment. But here's the truth: you can handle it. You've handled everything else life has thrown at you. You'll handle this too.

When you strip away the fear of failure, the right choice becomes obvious. Not easy. But obvious.

For more on this, You Are a Badass at Making Money by Jen Sincero isn't just about money - it's about getting out of your own way and trusting yourself enough to take action. It's funny, direct, and it'll kick your excuses to the curb. See on Amazon (paid link).

What To Do When You Make the "Wrong" Choice

You will make mistakes. You will sometimes follow your intuition and end up in a mess. That's not a sign that your intuition is broken. It's a sign that you're human.

When that happens, don't beat yourself up. Don't conclude that your intuition is unreliable. Instead, ask: "What was the signal I missed? What was my body trying to tell me that I didn't want to hear?"

Usually, the answer is that you ignored a small signal early on. A little heaviness. A slight resistance. You pushed through it because you wanted the outcome to be different. Your intuition was right. You just didn't listen.

That's not failure. That's data. Use it to get better at listening next time.

Building Trust With Yourself Over Time

Trust isn't built in a day. It's built in small moments. In the decision to choose the salad when your gut says your body needs it. In the decision to leave the party early when your energy is drained. In the decision to say no to something that looks good on paper but feels wrong in your body.

Every time you listen to your intuition and it works out, you build evidence. And evidence is what turns a "maybe I'm just making this up" into a "I know what I know."

Start keeping a journal. Nothing fancy. Just a notebook where you write down your intuitive hits and misses. Track the decisions you made with your gut. Track how they turned out. Over time, you'll see a pattern. You'll see that you're way more accurate than you give yourself credit for.

And if you want a structured way to do this, The Almanack of Naval Ravikant has a section on making decisions that's pure gold. Naval talks about how to separate your desires from your fears, and how to make choices that align with who you actually are - not who you think you should be. See on Amazon (paid link).

You Already Know How To Do This

Here's the thing you need to hear: you already have everything you need. Your intuition isn't something you need to learn. It's something you need to unlearn ignoring. You've been receiving signals your whole life. You just got trained to dismiss them as "anxiety" or "overthinking" or "being too sensitive."

You're not too sensitive. You're perfectly calibrated. You just need to start trusting what you already know.

Start small. Start today. Make one decision with your gut. Notice what happens. And then do it again tomorrow. That's how you build the trust. That's how you stop second-guessing. That's how you start living from a place of knowing instead of a place of hoping.

You've got this. You always have.

Ready to take the next step? Take our free assessment to discover what's blocking your intuitive decision-making and get a personalized practice plan. Start here.
